content:
The agent had the whole repo. It still missed the dependency that mattered.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Tuesday’s outage was not exotic. A background worker started calling a new internal API the web tier had used for months. The agent had indexed the entire repository. It suggested the change confidently. Tests went green. Production timed out because the worker pool ran in a network segment the API firewall blocked.
The whole repo was in context. The dependency that mattered was not.
Context windows feel like omniscience until you are in the bridge call explaining why omniscience did not include the network diagram, the cached DNS TTL, or the fact that “internal” means three different security zones here.
What Context Actually Gives You
A large context window gives you text proximity. It does not give you operational history, political constraints, or scars from the last time someone moved a similar call.
It answers: what files mention this endpoint? It does not answer: which callers are allowed to reach it from where?
That gap is system understanding — the slow accumulation of diagrams, incidents, postmortems, and hallway warnings that never made it into markdown.
Agents read what is written. Organizations run on what is remembered unevenly.
I probably should have caught the worker issue in review. I trusted “the agent saw the repo” as a substitute for asking the network question out loud. Context window size became a reason to skip dumb human questions. The dumb human questions were the oracle.
Tuesday in Slow Motion
Timeline for the curious:
→ 09:12 deploy merges; CI green; author on vacation next day → 09:40 queue depth rises; no errors in app logs because requests never arrive → 10:05 support notices stalled exports; on-call assumes worker bug → 10:30 we find TCP timeouts to API host; curl from worker pod fails → 11:00 network team points at firewall rule from 2019 nobody documented in repo → 14:00 hotfix routes traffic through allowed segment; backlog clears overnight
The fix was one line and one meeting. The cost was a day of customer impact and another entry in the “why we do architecture review” slide deck that everyone will forget until next Tuesday.
The agent was not wrong about syntax. It was wrong about environment, which is where outages live.
Why Whole-Repo Context Creates False Confidence
When tools advertise entire-repository awareness, reviewers relax. The relaxation is rational psychologically and irrational technically.
Whole-repo context still misses:
→ External systems not in the repo → Infrastructure defined in another team’s Terraform → Rate limits agreed in email → Feature flags owned by product ops → Data contracts enforced only in warehouse jobs
Understanding spans repositories and departments. Context windows span tokens.
Teams that treat window size as a replacement for onboarding are outsourcing the wrong function. You still need humans who walked the graph edges physically — tcpdump, traceroute, reading the runbook that lies, calling the platform team without shame.
Building Understanding on Purpose
We added boring rituals after Tuesday:
→ Dependency maps updated when services change callers, not only when services change code → “Where does this run?” section mandatory in specs for cross-service calls → Staging environments that mirror network segmentation imperfectly but intentionally → Post-incident prompts stored as searchable notes, not only Slack scrollback → Review question on every cross-tier change: show me the packet path
None of that fits in a context window neatly. All of it fits in a culture that respects system understanding as capital.
Agents accelerate edits along edges you already understand. They do not reliably discover the edge exists.
The Lesson for Tooling Buyers
Buying larger context because outages happened is like buying longer keyboards because emails got longer. Maybe helpful. Not the category fix.
The fix is investing in understanding artifacts — diagrams, decision logs, runbooks tested under stress, juniors paired with seniors on incidents, mid-levels maintaining the map.
Context windows are accelerants on known territory. Tuesday reminded us we still need people who know which territory is known.
The Diagram That Was Not in the Repo
The firewall rule that burned us lived in a diagram on a wiki page last updated by someone who left in 2021. The agent never saw it. I had seen it once in onboarding and forgot until the network team mentioned it.
System understanding is partly institutional memory with poor search indexing. Context windows search code well. They do not search the hallway conversation where someone said “workers never call that API directly.”
We started storing short “edge notes” after incidents — one paragraph each, tagged by service pair. Not formal ADRs. Faster. Searchable. Required reading before cross-service changes. Cheap habit. Would have prevented Tuesday.
Training Understanding Deliberately.
Understanding grows when you watch someone debug live, not when you read another generated summary. Pair on incidents. Rotate juniors through on-call shadowing with debriefs that include network paths, not only stack traces.
Agents can prep the diff. Humans still need to prep the map. Budget time for map maintenance the way you budget time for dependency upgrades.
Tooling Hype vs Operational Reality.
Vendors will keep selling larger windows because larger is measurable. Understanding is harder to sell because it looks like headcount and tenure and boring documentation.
Buy tools if they help. Do not confuse indexed tokens with earned knowledge. Tuesday’s outage was expensive tuition. Pay it once by changing habits, not by buying a longer index.
Onboarding Under Agent Pressure.
New hires used to build understanding by fixing small bugs across layers. Agents let new hires ship large changes before the mental model forms. Speed without map is dangerous for them and for on-call.
We slowed onboarding intentionally: first month includes manual tracing exercises, shadowing incidents, updating one edge note, and one small hand-written fix per week even if agents could do it faster. The slowness is investment.
When the Agent Helps Anyway.
Agents help once the map exists. They help implement along known edges. They help draft docs from accurate notes. They help refactor inside boundaries you already trust.
Use windows as accelerants on understood territory. Use humans to expand territory. Mixing those roles is how Tuesday happens.
The Questions That Survived Tuesday.
We printed four questions on the wall near the deploy button:
→ Where does this run → Who else calls it → What fails closed → What is the rollback story
Agents answer the third sometimes. The first two live outside the repo more often than we admit. The fourth is where incidents become three-day stories.
Ask them out loud in review even when the window is full. Especially then.
Postmortems as Index.
Postmortems are the index context windows lack. Write them short. Link them from specs. Reference them in standup when similar changes appear. Institutional memory is a maintenance task, not a side effect of hiring.
Tuesday hurt. It hurt less the second time we almost made the same mistake because the edge note existed and someone read it before merge. That is understanding compounding — slow, unglamorous, cheaper than outages.
Do Not Confuse Access With Authority.
Having the whole repo in context feels like authority to change any edge. Authority in production still lives with teams, zones, flags, and contracts. The agent accessed files. It did not access the organizational graph.
Reviewers must hold that distinction when window size makes authors confident. Confidence without authority is how Tuesday repeats on a different service with a different firewall and the same root cause.
Carry the Map Outside the Window.
After Tuesday we started requiring a one-paragraph “edges” note for cross-service changes: network path, owning team, last incident link. The note lives in the spec, not in hope that the repo contains the truth.
Context windows will keep growing. Maps still need humans who update them when the firewall rule changes. Buy the tool. Maintain the map.
The repo is not the system. Write that on a sticky note if you must.
The Dependency the Window Could Not Feel
Tuesday’s outage was not a missing file. It was a missing relationship.
The agent had the checkout service repo. It did not have the tribal knowledge that inventory reservations were eventually consistent with a 15-minute reconciliation job that product still treated as immediate truth in the UI copy. The fix it proposed was locally elegant and globally wrong.
Context can include text. Understanding includes load-bearing myths, the ticket that was never filed, and the dashboard everyone trusts for the wrong reason. Until your operational memory is written down, a larger window only makes wrong answers longer.
For the production and system-design depth that context windows cannot ingest from a zip file, start with The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering.
If you want to practice the cross-layer reasoning Tuesday punished us for skipping, Froquiz has 10,000+ scenario questions across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, Python, Java, JavaScript, React, microservices, and Senior Dev Challenge drills built for real system edges — not whole-repo trivia.
→ Froquiz
